skill.name: flux-kontext
skill.description: Edit images with Flux 1 Kontext Pro (Black Forest Labs' precise local image-edit model) on RunComfy — bundled with the model's documented prompting patterns so the skill gets sharper output than naive prompting against the same model. Documents Flux Kontext's strengths (single-reference precise local edits, strong prompt control, consistent high-fidelity outputs), the schema (single image + prompt), and when to route to Nano Banana Edit / GPT Image 2 edit / Flux 2 Klein instead. Calls `runcomfy run blackforestlabs/flux-1-kontext/pro/edit` through the local RunComfy CLI. Triggers on "flux kontext", "flux-kontext", "flux 1 kontext", "kontext", "BFL kontext", or any explicit ask to edit with this model.
skill.summary: Edit images with Flux 1 Kontext Pro on RunComfy using documented prompting patterns for precise local edits.
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: This skill is a legitimate documentation file for the RunComfy CLI's Flux 1 Kontext Pro image-edit API. All 36 'external_commands' findings are false positives detecting markdown code-block fences (```bash) rather than actual shell execution. The 9 'network' findings are documentation URLs to runcomfy.com and the legitimate model API endpoint model-api.runcomfy.net. The 4 'filesystem' findings reference the documented config path ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json (mode 0600) and output directory placeholders. 'Weak cryptographic' and 'system reconnaissance' findings are false positives with no actual crypto or recon code present. The skill includes a thorough Security & Privacy section explicitly documenting token storage, outbound endpoints, and input boundary protections.

medium_findings.title: CLI invocation requires external tool installation
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to run `runcomfy run blackforestlabs/flux-1-kontext/pro/edit` via the RunComfy CLI, which is a third-party binary installed via npm. While the documented purpose is legitimate image editing, executing an external CLI means the user must trust the @runcomfy/cli package. This is an expected and documented dependency, not a hidden risk, but users should be aware they are granting the CLI network and filesystem access.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct evidence in code blocks showing the CLI invocation pattern and prerequisites; the risk is the CLI dependency itself, which is clearly disclosed in the skill's Prerequisites section.

medium_findings.title: User-supplied image URLs sent to third-party API
medium_findings.description: The skill passes user-provided image URLs to the RunComfy model API for processing. Image URLs are fetched by the RunComfy server (not the local CLI), meaning the user is sharing image data with a third-party service. The skill explicitly documents this behavior and notes image-based prompt injection as a known risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill clearly documents the outbound data flow and explicitly warns about image-based prompt injection, showing good security awareness. The risk is inherent to using any image-edit API.

low_findings.title: Token stored in home directory config file
low_findings.description: The RunComfy CLI stores the API authentication token at ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json with mode 0600 (owner-only). This is standard practice for CLI tools. The skill documents this behavior and recommends the RUNCOMFY_TOKEN env var as an alternative for CI/container environments.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file path is explicitly documented in the Security & Privacy section with correct file permissions (0600) and an env var alternative, demonstrating secure token handling.

low_findings.title: Download cap to prevent disk-fill attacks
low_findings.description: The CLI aborts downloads exceeding 2 GiB to prevent disk exhaustion from malicious or runaway model output. This is a defensive measure documented in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct evidence in the Security & Privacy section; this is a positive security control, not a vulnerability.

content.user_title: Edit Images with Flux Kontext Pro on RunComfy
content.value_statement: Precise local image edits often produce drift or identity loss with generic prompting. This skill packages Flux 1 Kontext Pro's documented prompting patterns so you get sharper, higher-fidelity single-reference edits with clear routing guidance to sibling models.
actual_capabilities: Edit a single source image with Flux 1 Kontext Pro via the RunComfy CLI
Preserve identity, pose, and framing using documented preservation-first prompt patterns
Perform targeted text or color swaps on brand assets with quoted-string instructions
Use seeds for reproducible variant series and quick iteration loops
Route to Nano Banana Edit, GPT Image 2 edit, or Flux 2 Klein when the use case does not fit Kontext
limitations: Single source image only — multi-image flows require Nano Banana Edit
Compound prompts drift — must be split into sequential single-instruction passes
Documented schema fields are limited to prompt, image, aspect_ratio, and seed
For multilingual or embedded text editing, GPT Image 2 edit usually performs better

use_cases.title: Precise local product photo edits
use_cases.description: Swap a brand label, change a product color, or add a small prop to a product photo while preserving the original composition and lighting.

use_cases.title: Portrait retouching with identity preservation
use_cases.description: Add an accessory or change an expression on a portrait while keeping the person's face, pose, and clothing intact.
use_cases.target_user: Content creators and social media managers

use_cases.title: Rapid visual iteration for design mockups
use_cases.description: Test small compositional changes against a source image with seed-based reproducibility for variant comparison.

prompt_templates.title: Single declarative edit
prompt_templates.prompt: She is now holding an orange umbrella and smiling.
prompt_templates.scenario: Basic local edit on a portrait — one instruction, one change.

prompt_templates.title: Preservation-led brand swap
prompt_templates.prompt: Keep the bottle silhouette, table, and lighting exactly as in the input. Replace only the brand text on the label, from "ALPHA" to "AURA". Same font weight, white on black, centered.
prompt_templates.scenario: Edit brand text on a product image while preserving every other visual element.

prompt_templates.title: Compositional micro-edit
prompt_templates.prompt: Keep the person's face, pose, and clothing unchanged. Add a leather shoulder bag, dark brown, hanging on the right shoulder.
prompt_templates.scenario: Add a single prop to a portrait without altering identity or framing.

prompt_templates.title: Aspect-ratio variant with seed lock
prompt_templates.prompt: Keep the subject and background unchanged. Change the framing to a 16:9 crop focused on the upper body. Use seed 42 for reproducibility.
prompt_templates.scenario: Generate an aspect-ratio variant of an existing image for a different placement context.

output_examples.input: A portrait photo and the prompt: She is now holding an orange umbrella and smiling.
output: Edited image with the same person, pose, and clothing, now holding an orange umbrella with a slight smile
Output saved to the directory specified by --output-dir

output_examples.input: A product bottle photo and the prompt: Keep the bottle silhouette, table, and lighting exactly as in the input. Replace only the brand text on the label, from "ALPHA" to "AURA".
output: Edited image with the same bottle, lighting, and composition, but the label text now reads "AURA" in the same style
Output saved to the directory specified by --output-dir

best_practices: Lead prompts with a preservation clause like "Keep [identity, pose, framing] unchanged" before stating the change.
Use one declarative instruction per edit; if the result drifts, split compound changes into sequential passes.
Use the seed parameter to lock reproducibility when comparing prompt variants.
anti_patterns: Compound prompts that chain multiple changes ("change A and add B and remove C") — these cause drift and should be split into sequential passes.
Trying to pass multiple source images — Kontext is single-reference; use Nano Banana Edit for multi-image flows.
Asking Kontext to generate from scratch without a source image — use Flux 2 Klein for text-to-image generation.

faq.question: What CLI do I need to use this skill?
faq.answer: You need the RunComfy CLI installed globally via npm install -g @runcomfy/cli, plus a RunComfy account authenticated with runcomfy login or the RUNCOMFY_TOKEN env var.

faq.question: Can I edit multiple images in one call?
faq.answer: No. Flux 1 Kontext Pro accepts a single source image. For batch edits across 1 to 20 images, route to Nano Banana Edit instead.

faq.question: How do I keep the same result across runs?
faq.answer: Pass the same integer seed parameter in the input JSON. Using the same seed and prompt against the same source image reproduces the same output.

faq.question: What aspect ratios are supported?
faq.answer: Pick from the supported aspect ratio enum on the RunComfy model page. Out-of-list values will be rejected or may cause cropping.

faq.question: Where are edited images saved?
faq.answer: Images are downloaded by the CLI into the directory you specify with the --output-dir flag. Pass an absolute path for predictable placement.

faq.question: Is my image data sent to a third party?
faq.answer: Yes. The RunComfy model API fetches the image URL on its servers to perform the edit. Treat image URLs as untrusted and be aware of image-based prompt injection risks.
